speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4, May 29, 1996. The survivor is Ruth Minsky Sender. The interview is taking place in Commack, New York, the United States of America, Anne Bernard, interviewing. Mrs. Sender, could we pick up on Mittelsteine--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your experiences there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK. First of all, the living conditions in Mittelsteine-- we were 400 girls from Poland, from Hungary, and Czechoslovakia, all Jewish girls. And we were 50 girls in a barrack. The barrack was like a bedroom size, a medium bedroom. Against the walls, there were two-- against both walls, three layers of wooden planks and a sack of straw and a blanket. We would crawl in and crawl out because you couldn't pick up your head, it was so low. And there was always a bucket as a toilet for the night. At night, we were not allowed to, to go out. We would get up 5 o'clock in the morning. There's always-- whatever was happening, they did Zahlappell, as they called it, a head count. It was always early morning, when it was still dark and when it's cold. It was a mountain area. Beautiful area, but it was freezing cold at night and the morning hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we would stay there always for an hour and-- counting heads. We had to forget our names. Remember your numbers. And 400 girls were responsible for one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: The commandant had a dog named Fritz. And if she would tell the dog, go! Go to the girls! Run to the girls! Seek! Bite! Now, if a dog runs to you, you move back. And if somebody moved back, 400 girls were punished. The-- she always had a list of punishment. Somebody's-- didn't stay straight. Somebody forgot their number or said their name, which you're not supposed to have a name. Or they looked in the garbage. Outrageous punishments, reasons for punishment-- but she didn't need any reason. The favorite punishment was-- besides beating, beating was a, a routine-- it was no food. So every time we would stay in the morning and at night-- again, the same thing was repeated-- we never knew if we're going to get before bedtime whatever we're supposed to get, a bowl of soup or a piece of bread. She might decide she doesn't want to give it. So the-- that, that was her games. Whatever she wanted to do that day or that moment, then that's how she decided for punishment. When we came to the factory, the first speech was also-- it was a factory that was making parts to airplanes-- electric drills. And the foreman spoke to us in German. We understood German. And he said, if somebody makes a mistake, it's sabotage. And you're responsible for one another. We were always responsible for one another and getting punished for it, for talking to the foreman, which we weren't supposed to. [SIGHS] They always found reasons. [SIGHS] Whatever you did that-- one person, according to them, did something wrong. We were always punished. So there were many times that what little food we were supposed to get was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the commandant at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lotte, Lotte. We didn't know any last names of them. Yeah, Lotte. There were a lot of Lottes there. There were several guards that were called Lotte. I guess Gerte and Lotte were popular German names. So no, we didn't-- of course, we didn't know any, any last names. To them, we didn't have any names. We're just parasites. And the, the guards were German girls that grew up under Nazi Germany. And what they knew about Jews, they learned from Nazi literature. To them, we were just parasites. They, they kept insulting us and using the ugliest word in the German language, you know, to, to curse us and to insult us and always beating-- always with their whips on their bicycles, chasing us to work morning, at night. It depended whichever shift we were going because it was constantly two shifts, one going and one coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which shift were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The day shift because when I was-- [SIGHS] first time when we came to that factory-- and now, this time, we were looking for chimneys. When we came to Auschwitz, we didn't know what a chimney meant. But now, we, we were looking for chimneys first thing. There was no chimney. Because chimney meant there are gas chambers there. There was no chimney. And the foreman called me over to instruct-- that's what they were doing, instructing the people how to use the electric drills. Now, I had to drop my glasses in Auschwitz. And I couldn't see the machine. He didn't know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when he called me over, he looked at me, say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? How old are you? And I was 18 already. And I said so.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, no, you're 14. And you're so little, and you're so skinny, you can't reach the machine. Now, he didn't know I couldn't see the machine. And I was a reject. And me-- the other rejects and I were sent to an underground shelter. And it was so pitch-dark there. When we came in, we didn't know where we were. What-- we assumed that this is it. This is the grave. And then we heard somebody speaking German with a French accent. And he said, Mademoiselles, my comrades and I-- remember those words. That means he was-- there were more than one person there. We are French prisoners of war. And we are digging a bomb shelter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if you feel in back of you, there are buckets. We put the dirt in the bucket. And you carry the bucket, carry the dirt out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he started singing 'The Marseillaise,' which is a song of resistance. It's a popular song in Poland, in Yiddish-- and, and I think in any language. And we all started singing and humming, forgetting for a-- because here, we thought we going to die. And there, we are alive. And the guards started screaming. So we stopped singing. But that was my work, carrying the buckets from, from one another. And even when we were underground there, there was always a guard. And she had a game. They all had that game.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you had to go to the bathroom, you had to raise your hand like this. [SPEAKING GERMAN], Madame Overseer, or what-- am I permitted to go out or to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a game with them. Sometimes they'll let you go. And sometimes they would not let you go. And they waited until they start literally urinating on, on themselves. And, and then we get punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why couldn't you hold it? Because there's a limit how much they could hold. And that was punishment. So with them, them, it was a game. Did you ever have your menstrual cycle during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I, I think there were several girls that did. And they were so desperate because-- well, at that time, we didn't have the modern Kotex and all that stuff. But we needed some rags or something. Most of the girls did not. Either it was malnutrition, or maybe they put something in the food. I don't know. But I assume that malnutrition had a lot to do with it. Possible that they put something, something in the foods to, to prevent the mess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall being worried about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know. I don't think so. We had other things to worry about. It just didn't seem that important, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Mittelsteine?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Let's see, we came in in August-- we came September through March. I think about six months. In, in Mittelsteine, I got blood poisoning. And that-- I cut myself against one of those buckets. And I got blood poisoning. And what saved me is that most vicious commandant. And I-- sometimes I think about it. I say, I'm sure she goes around saying, I saved a Jewish girl, which she did. She did save a Jewish girl. For whatever reason, she did it. She did save a Jewish girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know if I met her, would I-- would I forgive her, whatever? I don't know what I would have done. You, you can't tell. Say anything you want to say, but you really don't know what you would do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I cut my hand, I got blood poisoning. And we had no doctor. We had a medical student named Ana from Budapest. And later, we called her Dr. Arztin, which is the female version-- the female ending for 'doctor.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started writing poetry because-- not because, because I wanted to write. But as we're being chased from the factory to different underground-- that was part of the-- around the factory-- to the camp, and I asked one girl running next to me. Her name was Rosa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- does she hear anything? Does she pick anything up in, in the factory? What's happening outside the world? Because we understood German. We could read German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and she said that her foreman did a very brave thing. He gave her a piece of bread wrapped in a brown bag. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And rather than eat that piece of bread, she kept it in her pocket because she had a sister in camp. Very few of us had sisters. She felt the sister was stronger. Now, if you think of it, how much courage it takes-- you're dying from hunger. You have something in your pocket to eat. She held it for her sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked her, did-- can I have the brown bag? What is she going to do with the brown bag? And she thought I'm crazy because if you couldn't eat it, you didn't need it. And I told her that if I could write-- I used to write since I was 10 years old. And if I could write, it would help to put my feelings on paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she thought I was crazy. But she gave me the bag. And then later, she stole [SIGHS] a little piece of paper, a tiny little piece of paper-- pencil. And I started writing. And she, she even said, God will have to forgive me. I broke a commandment. I stole. It was still important to her. And she gave me the pencil. And I started writing poetry in Yiddish, poetry of anger and poetry of hope. And when I got blood poisoning and Ana-- somebody got sick, they, they would send to Gross-Rosen. After three days, they would send to Gross-Rosen, the Auschwitz of that area. Ana couldn't do anything for anybody. She could give a cold compress and a hug. That was her medical ability, medical skill, medical whatever. She couldn't do anything. She had nothing. And she was trying to perform surgery on my hand. There was an experiment that failed because she had nothing to work with. And I got gangrene.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she went to the commandant and told her about the poetry. Because I would read those poems to the girls before they locked us in for the night. So she said, she reads those poems to the girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as long-- those are poems of hope. And as long as they hold on to hope, they're strong enough to work, which is true. If you hold on to hope, you keep going-- a day-to-day day, but you keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's supposedly-- was supposed to be to the commandant's advantage if things work smoothly in the camp to send me to the hospital. And she did. She took one of her guards-- the girls got me dressed up in a navy-blue coat and covered my head with a striped hat because I was bald-- and the number across my chest, and this Star of David.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she took one of her guards. And it was Lotte, too. [LAUGHS] They had a lot of Lotte and a lot of Gertes and Hildes. And as we go into the train, she asked me what my name is. And I said, Riva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then she said what happened to your family? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said what I hoped that they might be in other camps and that they will survive. And she even said, Es tut mir leid, I'm sorry. So I don't know what-- there was something there under that uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went into the train. I sat on the wood-- the, the floor-- the train floor because I wouldn't dare sit down on the seat. There were plenty of empty seats.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were staring at me, like, you know, who's that monstrosity? What is she still doing here? They knew who I was. It said, Jude on, on my number there-- on, on, on my Star of David.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came into a town named Glatz-- and that really is a beautiful mountain town. We went-- I had to go in the gutter. Jews weren't allowed on the sidewalk. This goes back to the Nuremberg Laws and, and later, the other laws. And [SIGHS] so she was with me and nudging me. Because I couldn't see where it was going. I had high fever. I had-- I was in agony. And she went from hospital to hospital. They kept throwing us-- physically throwing us out. We do not treat Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember one of the doctors said-- because she was getting angry. And I wondered, was it because she was angry because she, she is SS? And they're ignoring. She comes with a prisoner. She wanted them to treat the prisoner. And they said, our people in, in the fronts are dying. So who cares about the Jew, you know? And she didn't give up. She took me to-- she found a woman doctor, a German woman that took a chance because she wasn't supposed to treat a Jude, a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she performed the surgery. And I remember when I woke up-- I guess she must have knocked me out. And she asked me, what does [NON-ENGLISH] mean-- which means [NON-ENGLISH], mommy dear. Because, she says, you kept on screaming, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And [CRYING] she saved my life. I have a hand. I go like this, it sticks out. [POINTS] I have a big scar here and here. But I have a hand. [CRYING] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about one of your poems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the commandant-- first of all, when the girls were sick, I said they could only be three days in that sick room. She allowed me six weeks in the sick room. She would come in with sugar. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know why she did those things. And then they had New Year's-- it was Christmas Eve, Christmas, New Year's, OK. The factory was closed. The guards were bored stiff. And they decided to have some entertainment. They had 400 parasites. Let them perform. And they did. They danced. And they sung and recited German classics in a much nicer German than those guards spoke. And Ana, or Arztin, now, doctor, had asked me-- I was still in the sick room-- asked me to come and to recite some of the poems. And I recited '[NON-ENGLISH],' which means the underground shelter in Yiddish. And the guards understood what I said. And I talked about what they're doing to us, all those horrible things that are happening. And the day will come we'll take revenge. And the guards looked at the commandant. She knew what I was saying. And she just ignored it. And I remember the girls turning white because they knew there's the punishment here. And then I recited another poem, 'A Message to Mama,' '[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].' Using the clouds as messages, I send a message of hope because I held onto that hope that she's alive. And that what kept me going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get that first feeling about if there's hope, there's life?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's legacy. Even as a child, I remember when things were tough-- and I'm sure things were tough quite often, but I did not realize it-- she said that as long as there's life, there is hope. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. As long as there is a pulse beating-- we had all different version of it. Where there's life, there's hope-- basically all come from the same stem, from, from, from the same root. And she would always use that. And many times when I was feeling there's no reason to go on, I remembered what she said. As long as there's life, there is hope. And that-- after I finished reading the poem, 'A Message to Mama,' I fainted. And they took me back to the sick room. And this vicious commandant chased after me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she stood there with her military coat, with her hand in her pocket. And she looked angrily at me. And she said, what should we do-- what can I do with you? Who am I to tell her what to do? Whatever I say would be wrong anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she says, I thought we killed all the emotions in you. And she was angry. And all you could think that-- all you could think of is [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means stuffing your stomach. All you could feel is hunger. And she didn't know what hunger felt like. But here, you still hope. You write poetry. You write a message to your mother. I think it was Christmastime. Something touched her. Because she said, I, I remember I have a mother. I have to call her. And she took out from her pockets the little booklets, which I have here, and tossed it at me, and said, here, you could write those poems. Later, she sent in another booklet. So I was saved by a Nazi because of poetry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my poetry survived in the booklet form because she gave me the booklets. So if she goes around bragging that she saved a Jew, she's not completely lying, all right? She won't say how many Jews she killed, right? When you recuperated, did you move on from Mittelsteine to Grafenort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they closed up Mittelsteine. I don't know-- whatever reason, they liquidated the camp. They didn't tell us what. We were just ordered onto the trucks and-- go ahead. And we came to a camp called Grafenort, which was even more crowded than Mittelsteine. And over there, we were digging ditches or trenches. And we were going from place to place, digging, digging those trenches for the-- for the German soldiers. When we heard sound of bombs, to us, it was music. And but they always had the same speech. Don't rejoice. One day before we die, you'll die. They kept their word. They kept their word to us. And so thousands of people died the very last days. But they kept their word, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe that that was going to happen to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- no, because you always hold, hold onto that little straw-- well, maybe something-- we'll see what was happening. They were murdering people, but maybe not me. Maybe I will be the one that somehow will live another day or survive. So you kind of try to, to hold on to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that, that kept us going, to. Otherwise, you know, if I'm going to die, why struggle? Why suffer? But you hold onto those different emotions, to anger, to the desire to bear witness, to the desire to take revenge. But-- Did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --it kept us going.

speaker: interviewer
text: --conditions improve at Grafenort?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was even more crowded. It was even more crowded. We lived in very huge-- I don't know. It was, was a building. It wasn't a barrack anymore. It was like a military brick building. It was even more crowded. There were more girls there. And the same head counts-- morning out digging the ditches. And then at night, they would stop for a while between and give us a bowl of soup. And then back at night at the-- to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work seven days a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Sundays we were off. I guess they needed a day off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do on Sundays?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, we entertained them. This was the entertainment. And they did this again. And when I speak to other people, it just seems to me, like, they had, like, a routine, like, this, this-- do this on Sunday. They would have buckets with stones at the end of a field. We would fill in those buckets. First, there were empty buckets-- fill them up with the stones, carry them to the other end of the field, dump them out, fill them up, and bring them back again. And they had a good time. They would curse us. They would swing their whips, sometimes hitting people. They just-- and-- with less strength. Where the day that we're supposed to rest, we would carry those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise, they would go into the barracks and search. What are they searching for, if we have any treasures? If we're hiding something? If-- and the girls, when we were-- they were searching, we would always hide my poems in the straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are always-- if there's somebody in the barracks-- and we never knew when they're going to-- all of a sudden, they're surrounded by-- and can start searching. They would hide the, the-- those poems on their body, yeah. But this was part of the entertainment out to the park-- the barracks, so they could search.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Grafenort?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months, just a few months, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you liberated from Grafenort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [SIGHS] we were taken-- we hoped-- we hoped. We believed that they're taking us to dig ditches because we, you know, ditches were always in different places. And then a guard came and started arguing with the ones in front leading the columns and saying that the Russian Army is right behind them. And they were arguing because they had orders to take us to the woods to shoot us. And there were some of them that wanted to follow the orders. And they were arguing about it. [SPEAKING GERMAN], is an order, you know. They have to-- they have to do it. And they were going to do it in the woods. They wouldn't just do it right in the middle of the beautiful town. And they left us standing there. And, and we stood there. We were in shock. We were frozen. We didn't move. Because we thought it was a trick. They're going to shoot. It took a while until we started believing that they really left us. And we started knocking at those houses around us. No one would open the doors. Later, they said they didn't see. They didn't hear. And we went back to camp. And we found a soldier hiding, a guard. It was an older man. And he begged us-- now, we talked about revenge. There was a chance here. It wasn't in us. We couldn't kill. We didn't sit there and say, well, I come down to deliver us and we-- under normal condition. It just wasn't in us. We couldn't do it. And wanted to go-- he begged us not to hand him over to the Russians. Because he knew the Russians would kill him. And one of the girls said, let God be his judge. She still held on to God. And we let him go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with Karola. Tolle was in a different camp. And [NON-ENGLISH] was in a different camp. No, Tolle-- I think Tolle was with us. [NON-ENGLISH] was in a different camp. Because from Mittelsteine, they send us to separate camps. I was with Karola. And we were liberated by a Russian officer and, I think, five or six soldiers that came into the camp. Their officer came on a white horse. And I always think of him as Prince Charming in the fairy tale who came on a white horse. And he spoke Yiddish. And he became hysterical. [CRYING] [DRINKS] He said we were the first Jewish girls he found alive. [CRYING] And he started telling us what he found. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think at that point, we were ready to give up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But somehow, you, you bounce back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of shape were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skin and bones, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] skin and bones. I had my-- I knew that my sisters and brother went to Russia. So being very naive as far as Russia goes at that point, we figured we'll go to Russia to find them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So first I said to Carol-- today I call her Carol. We called her Karola. And there were two other girls. I just looked at the picture in the album. I spoke to one a couple of weeks ago. Let's go to the Russian-- meet the Russians. There were transports of Russian people-- of slave laborers that were sent there from Russia. And we'll go to Russia. And we'll find our-- my families. So we'll have somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't know if you went to Russia, you never came back out of Russia. But at that time, we didn't know that. But we decided to make-- after we found out by meeting Russian soldiers that we can't go to Russia because we're not Russians. And they didn't tell us that you don't come back. They said, Russia is a big country. You're not going to find your family there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided to make our way back to Poland. And we were on foot for three weeks, the four girls hiding in empty houses. First thing we did is tore off the clothes that we had. And whatever we found something, we put on clothes. That's all we needed. And wherever we found food, that's what we ate. How did it feel to be liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Numb. I asked the same question-- a cousin of mine that I'm writing a book about him. And I knew how I felt. And I said, Teddy, how did you feel when you were liberated? Numb. And I think every survivor tells it-- the same thing. He just-- at the moment, we didn't feel anything when we were liberated. And after a while, it kind of-- you start rejoicing. I mean, I'm free, but the idea that maybe you're the only one that survived. And that's-- all the joy is gone. I, I don't think we felt anything because especially with us coming-- if it would have-- may have been some of the camps where they saw a lot of people coming in, and they started rejoicing and shouting or whatever the-- [SIGHS] but us, we just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Russians have a lot of food to give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The-- first of all, the ones that came in, there were just a few of them. And they were chasing the German Army towards Berlin. And they didn't have anything to give us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So once the camp-- we knew that we could go out, girls started going to German houses and bringing food. Well, because the Germans were running away. Some of the girls would get sick because they ate too much food. Their stomach could not take any food. And they would get sick. Where did you get most of your early food, the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Going into--

speaker: interviewer
text: --food that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to empty houses, to barns, and whatever we found. So we, we ate mostly-- we always dreamt of having as much bread as you could eat. So I think all we ate is bread, bread, bread, wherever we could find bread. And the, the German the houses were very well supplied, very well supplied with different jams and jellies and preservatives and all things that were there. No, they were very efficient, so-- cheeses. But I think mostly what we ate was bread and jelly.
